summary: Plum-garden discussion with Liu Bei — names the great men of the age, dismisses each, then 'You, Imperial Uncle, and I' as the only heroes in the realm. Liu Bei drops his chopsticks; thunder covers the slip. Cao Cao laughs it off; the chapter's defining set piece.

Survives Cao Cao's heroes test by pretending fear of thunder; uses the Yuan Shu-versus-Yuan Shao march as cover to leave Xuchang with 50,000 troops, then slays Che Zhou and retakes Xuzhou. The escape that begins his anti-Cao Cao career.
Slays Che Zhou outside the Xuzhou gate when Liu Bei retakes the city — the act that publicly breaks Liu Bei's pretense of loyalty to Cao Cao.
With Liu Bei in the retaking of Xuzhou — exterminates Che Zhou's family as the brothers consolidate the city.
Che Zhou; Cao Cao's appointed Governor of Xuzhou; lured out under a false night-banner by Chen Deng and slain at the moat by Guan Yu.
Dong Cheng; brings the belt-decree to Liu Bei at his lodging in Xuchang; Liu Bei signs the pledge.
Wang Zifu; co-conspirator with Dong Cheng at the chapter's belt-decree meetings.
Wu Zilan; co-conspirator with Dong Cheng.
Ma Teng; signs the belt-decree pledge with Dong Cheng before returning to Xiliang.
Chen Deng; engineers the night-banner ruse that lures Che Zhou out of Xuzhou to be killed.
Chen Gui; with his son Chen Deng in the retaking of Xuzhou.
Cheng Yu; warns Cao Cao not to release Liu Bei from Xuchang; ignored.
Cheng Yu (duplicate record).
Guo Jia; warns Cao Cao that letting Liu Bei out is a fatal mistake; ignored.
Xun Yu; in Xuchang; warns Cao Cao against the heroes-test interview.
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Xu Chu; carries Cao Cao's invitation that brings Liu Bei to the plum-garden meeting.
Ji Ling; engages Liu Bei's vanguard when Yuan Shu's column tries to push through Xuzhou; killed by Zhang Fei in the ambush.

Yuan Shu; the imperial pretender, broken and reduced to fleeing north hoping to deliver the seal to his cousin Yuan Shao. Killed off-page after vomiting blood in the wilderness when his honey-water has run out — the chapter's closing image.
Yuan Yin; Yuan Shu's nephew; flees north with the remnants of the imperial-pretender court.
Lu Zhao; Yuan Shu officer named in the dying-empire scenes.
Zhu Ling; Cao Cao officer sent with Liu Bei against Yuan Shu — reports back when Liu Bei refuses to return.
Zhao Yun; reappears riding back into Liu Bei's life after Gongsun Zan's collapse — formally enters Liu Bei's service for good.
Gongsun Zan; named in passing — has just been destroyed by Yuan Shao at Yi Capital, his pyre offstage.
Zhang Yan; Black Mountain bandit chief named in passing in the post-Gongsun-Zan recap.
Zhang Yan (duplicate record).
Zhang Yan (duplicate record).
Zhang Yan (duplicate record).
Zhang Yan (duplicate record).
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in the Yuan Shao /Gongsun Zan finale recap.
Yuan Shao; finishes Gongsun Zan offstage and inherits the entire north; receives Liu Bei's outgoing alliance overtures via Sun Qian and Zheng Xuan.
Yuan Shao (duplicate record).
Mi Zhu; with Liu Bei in the retaking of Xuzhou.
Sun Qian; carries Liu Bei's letter via Zheng Xuan to Yuan Shao asking for alliance.
Sun Qian (duplicate record).
Man Chong; named in passing in the Xuchang court.
Chen Lan; minor figure named in passing in the Yuan Shu remnant roll.
Liu Biao; named in passing in the regional roll.
Liu Zhang; named in passing in the regional roll.
Sun Jian; named in passing as Sun Ce's late father.
Sun Ce; named in passing as the rising southern power.
Han Sui; named in passing as Ma Teng's western ally.
Zhang Liao; serves under Cao Cao.
Zhang Lu; named in passing as the Hanzhong Daoist ruler.
Zhang Xiu; named in passing — about to surrender to Cao Cao.
Zhang Xiu (duplicate record).
Zhang Xiu (duplicate record).
Zhang Xiu (duplicate record).
Mobilises against Liu Bei at Xuzhou while Yuan Shao gathers at the Yellow River; sends Liu Dai and Wang Zhong with 50,000 troops as the first probe. They lose and are captured by the brothers.
Liu Bei; receives Sun Qian's news that Yuan Shao has agreed in principle to an alliance after Zheng Xuan's letter; sets the brothers to picket the Cao Cao probe and welcomes the relief alliance.
Defeats and captures Wang Zhong in single combat outside Xuzhou — but releases him at Liu Bei's request to carry back the message that the brothers want peace.
Defeats Liu Dai by feigning retreat then ambushing him; captures Liu Dai alive and brings him back to camp.
Liu Dai; Cao Cao's probe commander; captured by Zhang Fei in single combat after a feint.
Liu Dai (duplicate record).
Wang Zhong; Cao Cao's co-probe commander; captured by Guan Yu in single combat.
Wang Zhong (duplicate record).
Zheng Xuan; classical scholar; writes the letter to Yuan Shao on Liu Bei's behalf — the diplomatic move that triggers Yuan Shao's intervention.

Chen Lin; pens the famous manifesto against Cao Cao for Yuan Shao — denouncing him as a 'eunuch's offspring' and listing the regime's crimes. The manifesto so cuts that Cao Cao reads it through his fever and laughs that it cured his headache.
Yuan Shao; finally moves south against Cao Cao with 700,000 troops; council split between Tian Feng's caution and Guo Tu / Shen Pei's push for war.
Yuan Shao (duplicate record).
Tian Feng; argues Yuan Shao should wait, build up the territory, and avoid pitched battle. For his caution he is jailed.
Ju Shou; backs Tian Feng's cautious strategy; also pushed aside in the Guandu mobilisation.
Shen Pei; Yuan Shao adviser pushing for an immediate strike on Xuchang.
Guo Tu; Yuan Shao adviser pushing for war alongside Shen Pei.
Yan Liang; named as Yuan Shao's leading champion — will lead the Baima van in the next chapters.
Wen Chou; named alongside Yan Liang as Yuan Shao's other champion.
Zhang He; named among Yuan Shao's named generals as the campaign mobilises.
Gao Lan; named among Yuan Shao's named generals.
Chunyu Qiong; named as Yuan Shao's commissariat commander.
Han Meng; named in Yuan Shao's order of battle.
Xu You; named among Yuan Shao's advisers.
Xu You (duplicate record).
Xu You (duplicate record).
Xun Shen; named among Yuan Shao's officers.
Zang Ba; named among the post-Lu Bu Xuzhou veterans drifting toward Cao Cao.
Che Zhou; named in passing as the recently-dead governor of Xuzhou.
Kong Rong; argues at Cao Cao's court against the Yuan Shao campaign — overruled.
Cao Hong; serves under Cao Cao at the Yellow River frontier.
Cao Hong (duplicate record).
Cao Ren; serves under Cao Cao at the Yellow River frontier.
Li Dian; serves under Cao Cao at the Yellow River frontier.
Yu Jin; serves under Cao Cao at the Yellow River frontier.
Cheng Yu; advises Cao Cao on the Guandu strategy.
Cheng Yu (duplicate record).
Xun Yu; running Xuchang during the mobilisation.
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Mi Zhu; with Liu Bei at Xuzhou.
Mi Fang; Mi Zhu's brother; defending Xuzhou as Liu Bei's auxiliary.
Sun Qian; carries the secret letter back from Zheng Xuan / Yuan Shao announcing the alliance.
Sun Qian (duplicate record).
Jian Yong; with Liu Bei at Xuzhou.
Lady Gan; Liu Bei's wife, kept in Xiapi as the Cao Cao army closes in.
Lady Mi; Liu Bei's other wife, in Xiapi with Lady Gan.
Chen Deng; named in passing in the Xuzhou retake recap.
Gongsun Zan; named in passing as the recently-immolated northern lord.
Dong Zhuo; named in passing in Chen Lin's manifesto enumerating Cao Cao's crimes.
He Jin; named in passing in Chen Lin's manifesto.

Mi Heng's debut — eccentric scholar Kong Rong recommends. Cao Cao puts him to drum duty at a court banquet; Mi Heng strips naked and beats the drum so contemptuously the court is paralysed, then walks out reviling Cao Cao name by name. Cao Cao sends him to Liu Biao who passes him to Huang Zu — who kills him for the same disrespect.

Cao Cao; humiliated by Mi Heng's naked diatribe; sends him to Liu Biao to be rid of him. Survives Ji Ping's headache-tea poisoning attempt — Ji Ping smashes his own brow against the pillar in defiance after the plot is exposed.
Dong Cheng; recruits the imperial physician Ji Ping into the belt-decree conspiracy after Ji Ping volunteers.
Kong Rong; introduces Mi Heng to Cao Cao on the strength of Mi Heng's wit — the recommendation that triggers the naked-drumming scandal.
Liu Biao; receives Mi Heng from Cao Cao and, knowing the trap, passes him on to Huang Zu, where Mi Heng is killed for the same insolence.
Huang Zu; kills Mi Heng with a sword stroke when Mi Heng compares him in the abuse to 'a wooden idol in a temple'.
Kuai Liang; with Liu Biao when Mi Heng is forwarded; counsels passing him along to Huang Zu.
Jia Xu; surrenders with Zhang Xiu to Cao Cao in this chapter — Cao Cao welcomes him so warmly that Jia Xu becomes one of his most-trusted advisers from here on.
Jia Xu (duplicate record).
Zhang Xiu; surrenders to Cao Cao with Jia Xu's recommendation — Cao Cao welcomes him despite having killed Cao Ang and Dian Wei at Wancheng.
Zhang Xiu (duplicate record).
Zhang Xiu (duplicate record).
Zhang Xiu (duplicate record).

Yang Xiu's debut — Cao Cao's brilliant secretary; the famous 'one cup of strained curds' / 'one man one mouthful' anecdote and the 'wisdom-equals-mine' aside foreshadow his eventual execution at Hanzhong.
Yang Xiu (duplicate record).
Ma Teng; signed the belt-decree but has now returned to Xiliang; remains in the conspiracy in absentia.
Wang Zifu; among the belt-decree conspirators.
Wu Zilan; among the belt-decree conspirators.
Liu Bei; off-stage in this chapter at Xuzhou; awaiting Yuan Shao's intervention.
Liu Ye; counsels Cao Cao at court — advises receiving Zhang Xiu and Jia Xu warmly.
Cheng Yu; with Cao Cao at court.
Cheng Yu (duplicate record).
Guo Jia; with Cao Cao at court.
Xun Yu; running Xuchang.
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Xun You; with Cao Cao.
Cao Ren; serves under Cao Cao.
Li Dian; serves under Cao Cao.
Xiahou Dun; serves under Cao Cao.
Yu Jin; serves under Cao Cao.
Xu Chu; Cao Cao's bodyguard captain.
Xu Huang; serves under Cao Cao.
Zhang Liao; serves under Cao Cao.
Han Song; minor figure named in passing in the Jingzhou roll.
Han Sui; named in passing — Ma Teng's western ally.
Man Chong; at Cao Cao's court.
Wang Zhong; released after capture in chapter 22; reappears here at Cao Cao's court being berated for the failed probe.
Wang Zhong (duplicate record).
Liu Dai; released after capture in chapter 22; reappears at Cao Cao's court alongside Wang Zhong.
Liu Dai (duplicate record).
Yuan Shao; off-stage gathering at the Yellow River as the chapter unfolds at Xuchang.
Yuan Shao (duplicate record).

Belt-decree exposed via a stable-hand betrayal; raids Dong Cheng's household, finds the decree and pledge, and exterminates all five conspirators with their entire clans (~700 people). Goes into the palace sword in hand and personally strangles the pregnant Consort Dong as the emperor and empress watch — the chapter's defining cruelty. Then marches against Liu Bei at Xuzhou.
Dong Cheng; whole household executed when the belt-decree is found at his home. Goes to his death without recanting.
Wang Zifu; killed with Dong Cheng's group.
Consort Dong; Dong Cheng's sister; five months pregnant when Cao Cao drags her from the emperor's apartments and strangles her with a silken cord. The reigning emperor and empress watch in silence.
Ma Teng; named at Xiliang — out of reach of the immediate purge, but now an open enemy.

Defeated by Cao Cao's overwhelming attack on Xuzhou; loses contact with Guan Yu (now in Xiapi), Zhang Fei (fled to Mangdang Hills), and his wives. Flees alone north to Yuan Shao via Mi Zhu and Sun Qian.
Loses contact with Liu Bei in the chaos; falls back on Xiapi to defend Liu Bei's wives. The setup for his three conditions in the next chapter.
Flees to the Mangdang Hills with a remnant of troops when Xuzhou collapses.
Chen Deng; among the surrendered Xuzhou officials when the city falls.
Mao Jie; coordinates the move on Xuzhou from Xuchang.
Yuan Shao; receives Liu Bei with all honour at Jizhou — Liu Bei has lost his army but gains the moral high ground as the imperial uncle who signed the belt-decree.
Yuan Shao (duplicate record).
Yuan Tan; Yuan Shao's eldest son; welcomes Liu Bei at the Pingyuan border on his father's orders.
Tian Feng; counsels Yuan Shao from his cell to strike now while Cao Cao is committed at Xuzhou — Yuan Shao refuses, citing the illness of his youngest son.
Cao Hong; serves under Cao Cao at Xuzhou.
Cao Hong (duplicate record).
Xiahou Dun; commands the assault on Xuzhou.
Xiahou Yuan; commands the assault on Xuzhou.
Li Dian; in the Xuzhou assault.
Yu Jin; in the Xuzhou assault.
Xu Chu; Cao Cao's bodyguard at Xuzhou.
Xu Huang; in the Xuzhou assault.
Zhang Liao; in the Xuzhou assault.
Cheng Yu; advises Cao Cao on the Xuzhou strategy.
Cheng Yu (duplicate record).
Guo Jia; advises Cao Cao.
Xun Yu; running Xuchang during the campaign.
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Mi Zhu; flees Xuzhou north to Liu Bei in Jizhou.
Sun Qian; flees with Mi Zhu to Liu Bei in Jizhou.
Sun Qian (duplicate record).
Jian Yong; with Liu Bei on the flight north.
Liu Dai; named in passing in the Xuzhou recap.
Liu Dai (duplicate record).
Dong Zhuo; named in passing in the chapter recap of the belt-decree's lineage of court murder.

Cornered at Tushan after a feigned-retreat trick by Zhang Liao; accepts Cao Cao's three conditions ('I surrender to Han, not Cao Cao; the ladies must be respected; the day I learn where Liu Bei is, I leave at once'). Relieves Baima by riding alone into Yan Liang's line and decapitating him with one stroke before he can react — the most-cited single-combat in the novel.
Accepts Guan Yu's three conditions on Zhang Liao's recommendation and lavishes gifts on him to win his loyalty. Gets Guan Yu's saber-thrust against Yan Liang as the immediate dividend at Baima.
Zhang Liao; rides to Tushan and negotiates Guan Yu's surrender terms — his repayment of the debt for being spared at the White Gate Tower.

Yan Liang; Yuan Shao's champion at Baima; cuts down Cao Cao's first three duelists in succession; killed by Guan Yu in a single saber-stroke before he can even draw his sword. The chapter's signature kill.
Liu Bei; off-stage at Jizhou; learns of Guan Yu killing Yan Liang and realises his brother has gone over to Cao Cao. Yuan Shao threatens to execute Liu Bei — defused by Liu Bei's careful answer.
Yuan Shao; orders Liu Bei's execution when news of Yan Liang's death arrives, then reverses it when Liu Bei talks his way clear. Sends Wen Chou to Yan Crossing in revenge.
Yuan Shao (duplicate record).
Ju Shou; reads the heavens and warns Yuan Shao against the river crossing; ignored.
Tian Feng; still jailed; not consulted as Yuan Shao orders the Yan Crossing campaign.
Lady Gan; Liu Bei's wife; protected by Guan Yu under Cao Cao's terms.
Lady Mi; Liu Bei's other wife; protected by Guan Yu under Cao Cao's terms.
Song Xian; Cao Cao officer (former Lu Bu lieutenant); Yan Liang cuts him down at Baima.
Wei Xu; Cao Cao officer; killed by Yan Liang at Baima alongside Song Xian.
Xu Huang; engages Yan Liang at Baima and is forced back after 20 bouts — only Guan Yu's strike succeeds.
Xiahou Dun; commands part of the Cao Cao army at Baima.
Xu Chu; Cao Cao's bodyguard at Baima.
Cheng Yu; designs the Tushan feigned-retreat that draws Guan Yu out of position.
Cheng Yu (duplicate record).
Xun Yu; in Xuchang.
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Xun Yu (duplicate record).
Zhang Fei; off-stage in the Mangdang Hills.
